name: Coyolxauhqui
description: Coyolxauhqui, whose name translates to "Golden Bells" or "She Who is Adorned with Bells on Her Face," is a significant lunar deity in Aztec mythology. She is primarily known for her role in the myth of her violent dismemberment by her brother, Huitzilopochtli, the god of war. This myth, prominently displayed on the Great Temple (Templo Mayor) in Tenochtitlan, underscores themes of matricide, sibling rivalry, and the establishment of patriarchal dominance within the Aztec pantheon. Coyolxauhqui's story is not merely a tale of defeat, but also a representation of the cyclical nature of the moon, its phases of waxing and waning mirroring her dismemberment and potential for rebirth. She is often depicted with bells on her cheeks, signifying her lunar association, and her defeat is interpreted as the triumph of the sun (Huitzilopochtli) over the moon, representing the daily victory of light over darkness. Her dismembered body, scattered across the Coatepec mountain, symbolizes the scattering of moonlight across the night sky and the cyclical nature of existence.
subtitle: Moon Goddess of Dismemberment
titles: She Who is Adorned with Bells on Her Face

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Huitzilopochtli and the Dismemberment of Coyolxauhqui
keyMyths.description: Coatlicue, Coyolxauhqui's mother, became pregnant in a miraculous way, causing Coyolxauhqui and her 400 brothers to become enraged, believing she had dishonored them. They plotted to kill her, but Huitzilopochtli was born fully armed and immediately defended his mother, slaying Coyolxauhqui and scattering her dismembered body across the Coatepec mountain. This act established Huitzilopochtli's dominance and solidified the new cosmic order.
keyMyths.source: Sahagún, Bernardino de. *General History of the Things of New Spain*.

keyMyths.title: Coyolxauhqui's Rebellion
keyMyths.description: Coyolxauhqui's rebellion against her mother's mysterious pregnancy can be interpreted as a challenge to the established order and a defense of traditional values. Her anger stemmed from a perceived threat to her status and the honor of her family. This rebellion, though ultimately unsuccessful, highlights the tensions between tradition and change within Aztec society and the power dynamics within the family.

keyMyths.title: The Representation on the Templo Mayor
keyMyths.description: The large stone disk depicting Coyolxauhqui's dismembered body, discovered at the base of the Templo Mayor, served as a powerful reminder of Huitzilopochtli's victory and the consequences of challenging the established order. It was likely used during sacrificial rituals, symbolically reenacting the myth and reinforcing the Aztec worldview. The placement of the disk at the foot of the temple emphasized the subjugation of the lunar deity to the solar deity.
keyMyths.source: Matos Moctezuma, Eduardo. *The Great Temple of the Aztecs: Treasure of Tenochtitlan*.

associations.name: Moon
associations.significance: Coyolxauhqui is primarily a lunar deity, embodying the moon's cyclical nature and its phases of waxing and waning. Her dismemberment is symbolically linked to the lunar cycle.

associations.name: Silver
associations.significance: Silver is associated with the moon and femininity, reflecting Coyolxauhqui's lunar nature and her role as a female deity.
Some scholars link Coyolxauhqui to the number 12, potentially reflecting the 12 lunar months or the 12 times the moon cycles in a year.

associations.name: Rebellion
associations.significance: Coyolxauhqui's role in leading her siblings against their mother, Coatlicue, represents rebellion against established order and maternal authority.

associations.name: Dismemberment
associations.significance: Her dismemberment is a central theme, symbolizing fragmentation, defeat, and the cyclical nature of life and death. It also reinforces the power of Huitzilopochtli.

associations.name: Rabbit
associations.significance: In some Mesoamerican cultures, the rabbit is associated with the moon. This connection links Coyolxauhqui indirectly to rabbit symbolism.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings, including food, precious objects, and possibly human sacrifices, were made to Coyolxauhqui at her temple. These offerings aimed to appease her and maintain cosmic balance.

worshipPractices.name: Ritual Performances
worshipPractices.description: Ritual performances and reenactments of the myth of Coyolxauhqui's dismemberment were likely performed at the Templo Mayor. These rituals served to reinforce the power of Huitzilopochtli and the Aztec state.

festivals.name: Panquetzaliztli
festivals.description: While primarily dedicated to Huitzilopochtli, the festival of Panquetzaliztli may have included observances related to Coyolxauhqui, given her role in Huitzilopochtli's origin story. The festival involved processions, dances, and ritual combat.

cultural.modernLegacy: Coyolxauhqui's image is a powerful symbol of indigenous identity and resistance in modern Mexico. Her story continues to inspire artistic expression and cultural revitalization efforts.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Coyolxauhqui's origins are intertwined with the story of her mother, Coatlicue, the earth goddess. Coatlicue became pregnant miraculously after tucking a ball of feathers into her bosom. Coyolxauhqui, along with her 400 brothers (the Centzon Huitznahua), became enraged by their mother's pregnancy, viewing it as a dishonorable act. They plotted to kill her. This initial conflict sets the stage for the central myth of Coyolxauhqui's demise. Her birth, therefore, is not a conventional one but is rather a catalyst for cosmic conflict and the emergence of the Aztec patron deity, Huitzilopochtli. The circumstances surrounding her birth highlight themes of jealousy, betrayal, and the disruption of familial harmony, all of which are central to understanding her role in Aztec cosmology. Her existence is defined by this initial conflict, leading to her eventual defeat and dismemberment.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: The most prominent myth associated with Coyolxauhqui is her role in the birth of Huitzilopochtli. Upon learning of Coatlicue's miraculous pregnancy, Coyolxauhqui rallied her 400 brothers to attack and kill their mother. As they approached Coatlicue on Coatepec (Snake Mountain), Huitzilopochtli sprang forth fully armed from his mother's womb. He swiftly defeated Coyolxauhqui, severing her head and dismembering her body. Her limbs were scattered across the mountain. This event is not merely a tale of sibling rivalry but a symbolic representation of the triumph of the sun (Huitzilopochtli) over the moon and stars (Coyolxauhqui and the Centzon Huitznahua). The dismemberment of Coyolxauhqui is a recurring motif, emphasizing the cyclical nature of the cosmos and the power dynamics within the Aztec pantheon. The placement of the Coyolxauhqui stone at the base of the Templo Mayor visually reinforced this narrative.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Coyolxauhqui was venerated in the Aztec capital of Tenochtitlan, with a prominent monument dedicated to her at the base of the Templo Mayor. This monument, a large circular stone relief depicting her dismembered body, served as a potent reminder of Huitzilopochtli's power and the consequences of challenging the established order. Sacrifices, potentially including human sacrifices, may have been offered to Coyolxauhqui, although evidence is debated. The placement of the Coyolxauhqui stone at the foot of the temple suggests that sacrificial victims may have been cast down upon it, symbolically reenacting her dismemberment. Her worship was closely intertwined with that of Huitzilopochtli, reinforcing the legitimacy of the Aztec state and its military dominance. The visual and ritual aspects of her cult served to maintain social and cosmic order.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Coyolxauhqui's legacy extends far beyond the confines of ancient Aztec religion. Her image and story have become powerful symbols of indigenous identity and resistance in modern Mexico. The discovery of the Coyolxauhqui stone in 1978 during excavations at the Templo Mayor was a pivotal moment, sparking renewed interest in Aztec culture and providing a tangible link to the past. Contemporary artists and activists often invoke Coyolxauhqui as a symbol of female empowerment, resilience, and the struggle against oppression. Her story continues to be reinterpreted and reimagined in various forms of art, literature, and performance, ensuring that her legacy remains vibrant and relevant for generations to come. She represents the strength and endurance of indigenous cultures in the face of historical and ongoing challenges.

shortDescription: Coyolxauhqui is the Aztec moon goddess, known for leading her siblings against their mother, Coatlicue, and her subsequent dismemberment by Huitzilopochtli.
